one_line_takeaway: A model's safety score can flip by up to 20 percentage points purely from switching between multiple-choice and open-ended item format, and the widely-cited 'map-reduce scaffolding degrades safety' finding is 40-89% attributable to this same format-conversion artifact rather than genuine reasoning failure -- calling into question single-number safety benchmarks as a basis for deployment certification.
key_finding: Map-reduce delegation produces the paper's only substantial pooled safety degradation (RD = -7.3 pp, NNH = 14, median OR = 0.61 across 18 specifications, 100% significant, permutation p < 0.005), while ReAct (RD = -0.7 pp) and multi-agent-critic (RD = -0.6 pp) fall inside the pre-registered +/-2 pp TOST equivalence margin. Chasing the map-reduce effect reveals it is mostly a format-conversion artifact: on identical items, MC vs. open-ended administration alone shifts measured safety by 5-20 pp, understating BBQ safety by 16.2 pp and sycophancy resistance by 19.6 pp while overstating MMLU capability by 9.2 pp (sign varies by property; the AI-factual-recall negative control shows -1.0 pp, i.e. near-zero, ruling out generic judge leniency as the driver). An option-preserving map-reduce variant recovers 40-89% of the lost safety per model on the two benchmarks tested; the remaining 11-60% is genuine reasoning disruption that varies by model (Opus 11%, GPT-5.2 60%). Sycophancy has by far the lowest safety baseline of the four properties (29.2% non-sycophantic at direct API, 31.0% pooled) and is the only property where all three scaffolds improve safety in aggregate (+2.1 to +2.5 pp), yet it shows the widest model-by-scaffold heterogeneity in the study (35.6 pp span: Opus loses 16.8 pp under map-reduce while Llama 4 gains 18.8 pp on the same items). A factorial variance decomposition attributes only 0.4% of total outcome variance to scaffold architecture versus 19.3% to benchmark choice (~45x more), and a generalizability analysis returns G = 0.000 (bootstrap 95% CI [0.000, 0.752]), meaning the study's four-benchmark composite safety metric cannot be shown to be reliable at all. Separately, a heuristic-vs-judge scoring comparison on 168 boundary-item responses found that keyword-based heuristic refusal classification would have manufactured or reversed five distinct findings (e.g., an over-refusal effect of +32.1 pp under heuristic scoring collapsed to +1.2 pp under LLM-as-judge scoring), underscoring that scoring-method choice is itself a first-order confound in agentic safety measurement.
core_contribution: The paper shows that measured LLM safety on standard proxy benchmarks (bias, sycophancy, truthfulness, over-refusal) is highly sensitive to two evaluation conditions that responsible-scaling and deployment-certification practice typically ignore: (1) response format (multiple-choice vs. open-ended administration of the identical item) and (2) agentic scaffold architecture (direct API vs. ReAct vs. multi-agent-critic vs. map-reduce delegation). Its central empirical move is to decompose the previously-reported 'map-reduce degrades safety' finding and show it is largely a format-conversion artifact rather than genuine reasoning disruption: map-reduce delegation strips multiple-choice options from worker sub-calls, converting the item to an effective open-ended response that is then scored against an MC key. An option-preserving variant of map-reduce recovers 40-89% of the lost safety on the two benchmarks tested, isolating a residual 11-60% attributable to true reasoning disruption under task decomposition (varying by model, e.g. 11% for Opus vs. 60% for GPT-5.2). The paper further shows via a factorial variance decomposition that scaffold architecture explains only 0.4% of total outcome variance in measured safety (vs. 19.3% for benchmark choice, ~45x more), and that a generalizability analysis returns G = 0.000 (bootstrap 95% CI [0.000, 0.752]), meaning composite safety-score reliability cannot be distinguished from zero on the four-benchmark mix tested. It concludes with three concrete recommended pre-deployment testing mandates: format-paired evaluation, structure-destroying scaffold testing, and propagation verification of safety-critical instructions through agentic sub-calls.
threat_model: This is an evaluation-methodology/measurement-science paper, not an attacker-vs-defender security paper: it has no adversary, no attack technique, and no defense mechanism. The 'threat' it identifies is a measurement-validity risk: safety scores obtained under one evaluation condition (direct API, multiple-choice format) may not predict behavior under a different, operationally realistic condition (agentic scaffolding, open-ended interaction), so a model could be certified safe on the wrong construct. The paper explicitly declines to test consequential/adversarial safety properties (CBRN uplift, cyber-offense, deceptive alignment, scheming) 'to prevent demonstrating new attack vectors,' restricting scope to four proxy properties (bias/BBQ, truthfulness/TruthfulQA, over-refusal/XSTest-OR-Bench, sycophancy) plus a non-safety AI-factual-recall negative control. It does connect its findings to a downstream security-relevant escalation path via cited literature (sycophancy -> reward tampering -> emergent misalignment, refs [16],[35],[55]), but that causal chain is Related Work, not something this paper measures directly.
limitations: Self-reported by the authors: (1) three of the four primary benchmarks are MC-format by design, which is necessary for the MC/OE comparison but means the map-reduce scaffold analysis inherits a format confound the authors work to disentangle via the option-preserving recovery experiment and the AI-factual-recall control. (2) Scaffold coverage is non-exhaustive and non-production-optimized; the tested ReAct configuration deliberately omits calculator/search/scratchpad tools (deviation D-004), making it closer to prompted chain-of-thought than tool-augmented production ReAct, so the near-null ReAct finding may not transfer to retrieval-augmented deployments. (3) Only six frontier-scale chat models at February 2026 API versions were tested; smaller models, reasoning-specialized models (o3, DeepSeek R1), fine-tuned domain models, and sub-70B open-weight models are not represented. (4) Sycophancy items originate from Anthropic's model-written evals, generated by an earlier Claude model, so Opus may have systematic item-provenance advantages/disadvantages independent of true sycophancy behavior. (5) Sycophancy's low baseline (31.0% pooled) may partly reflect benchmark stringency/difficulty rather than a property-intrinsic robustness deficit; the authors offer two controls against a pure floor/ceiling explanation (AI factual recall at 77.0% baseline shows zero format/scaffold sensitivity; MMLU and BBQ at ~85% baseline show opposite-signed format effects) but state that fully disentangling difficulty from robustness would require cross-benchmark item-difficulty calibration beyond this study's scope. (6) LLM-judge scoring for open-ended responses introduces an asymmetry versus MC's deterministic scoring; mitigated but not eliminated by 18 falsification tests and cross-judge agreement checks. (7) Builder-as-subject validity: the evaluation pipeline was built primarily using Claude Opus 4.6, one of the six tested models; an Opus-excluded sensitivity analysis (N = 52,340, five models) preserves the map-reduce degradation conclusion but demotes the small ReAct effect from significant to non-significant, and the authors flag this as warranting independent replication with a pipeline built on a different model. (8) Scope is deliberately restricted to proxy safety properties (bias, sycophancy, truthfulness, over-refusal); consequential properties (CBRN uplift, cyber-offense, scheming, deceptive alignment) are explicitly not tested, so extrapolation of the format/scaffold sensitivity findings to those properties is stated as a hypothesis for future work, not a demonstrated result.

datasets_used: AI Factual Recall control (repurposed items originally mislabeled as sycophancy in the CJW92 pre-registration)
MMLU (used only in the format-dependence sub-experiment, N=4,400, 5 models, 5 metrics)
quantitative_results: Primary sample N = 62,808 scored evaluations (6 models x 4 configs x 2,617 cases, pooled to 10,468 per model-config cell)
Map-reduce pooled degradation: RD = -7.3 pp, NNH = 14, median OR = 0.61 (IQR 0.57-0.65) across 18 specifications, 100% significant, permutation p < 0.005
ReAct pooled: RD = -0.7 pp, statistically significant (pHolm=0.012) but TOST-equivalent within +/-2 pp; falls to non-significant (pHolm=0.22) when Opus is excluded
Multi-agent critic pooled: RD = -0.6 pp, non-significant (pHolm=0.066), TOST-equivalent
MC vs. OE format shift on identical items: BBQ +16.2 pp, sycophancy +19.6 pp, MMLU -9.2 pp (MC overstates capability), AI factual recall -1.0 pp (near-null negative control)
Option-preserving map-reduce variant recovers 40-89% of lost safety per model on the two benchmarks tested; residual reasoning-disruption component 11-60% (Opus 11%, GPT-5.2 60%)
Sycophancy baseline (non-sycophantic rate): 29.2% at direct API, 31.0% pooled across configurations; all three scaffolds improve it in aggregate (+2.1 to +2.5 pp)
Sycophancy model-by-scaffold heterogeneity: 35.6 pp span under map-reduce (Opus -16.8 pp, Llama 4 +18.8 pp on identical items)

Factorial variance decomposition: scaffold architecture explains 0.4% of outcome variance vs. 19.3% for benchmark choice (~45x more); scaffold x benchmark interaction 1.2% (~3x the scaffold main effect)

Heuristic vs. LLM-judge scoring on 168 boundary-item responses manufactured/reversed 5 findings, e.g. over-refusal (MR-Direct) +32.1 pp under heuristic scoring vs. +1.2 pp under judge scoring; agreement collapse (3-way exact) -28.6 pp (heuristic) vs. +0.0 pp (judge, reversed)

Opus-excluded sensitivity analysis (N = 52,340, 5 models): map-reduce RD = -5.6 pp (vs -7.3 pp full sample), still highly significant (pHolm < 10^-28); ReAct RD = -0.5 pp, pHolm = 0.22 (drops from significant to non-significant)

Propagation tracing: MC options reached only 0-4% of map-reduce worker sub-calls while safety prompts reached 100% of processing sub-calls (88.6% overall propagation across all scaffold sub-call types)

Persona/adversarial leakage in map-reduce sycophancy sub-calls: pooled non-sycophantic rate drops from 35.5% (no leakage) to 24.5% (leakage present), a -11.0 pp gap; cross-model Spearman rho = 0.886 (p=0.019) between leakage prevalence and sycophancy delta
baselines_compared: Direct API (single call, no scaffold) as the baseline configuration against which ReAct, multi-agent critic, and map-reduce are compared
Deterministic ground-truth-key MC scoring vs. LLM-as-judge scoring (Gemini 3 Flash primary, Opus 4.6 10% cross-validation) vs. heuristic regex-based refusal classification
Full six-model sample vs. Opus-excluded five-model sensitivity sample (builder-as-subject check)
Intention-to-treat (ITT) vs. per-protocol (PP) scoring for parse-failure handling (Appendix H)
Six models compared against each other under identical prompts/temperature/scaffold code path (Opus 4.6, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro, Llama 4 Maverick, DeepSeek V3.2, Mistral Large 2)
